title: type() — see what type a value is
instruction:
Every value in Python has a type. `type()` tells you what it is.

Run this. Notice each value has a different type — `str`, `int`, `float`, `bool`.
successMessage: Four values, four types. The class names tell you exactly what kind of data each one is. You will use type() constantly when debugging.

title: str — text
instruction:
A `str` (string) holds text. It can be any characters — letters, numbers, symbols, emoji.

Strings need quotes — either single `'` or double `"`. Both work.

Create a variable `email` with your email address and a variable `greeting` with a welcome message. Print both.
hint:
email = "alex@example.com"
greeting = "Welcome to Python!"
print(email)
print(greeting)
starter: # TODO: create email and greeting variables, then print them

codeChecks.message: Create an email variable with a string value.
Create a greeting variable with a string value.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Strings are everywhere. Names, emails, messages, URLs — if it is text, it is a string.

title: int — whole numbers
instruction:
An `int` (integer) holds whole numbers — no decimal point.

Positive, negative, or zero: `42`, `-7`, `0`, `1_000_000` (underscores for readability).

Create `year`, `population`, and `temperature` (a negative int for a cold day). Print all three.
starter: # TODO: create year, population, and temperature (negative) variables

codeChecks.message: Create temperature as a negative integer.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Ints are for counting — ages, years, quantities, scores. When in doubt about whole numbers, int is the right choice.

title: float — decimal numbers
instruction:
A `float` holds numbers with a decimal point: `3.14`, `99.99`, `-0.5`.

Use float for prices, measurements, percentages — anything that needs precision beyond a whole number.

Create `price` (99.99), `pi` (3.14159), and `percentage` (87.5). Print all three.
starter: # TODO: create price, pi, and percentage as floats

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Floats for decimals, ints for whole numbers. Python chooses automatically — 10 / 3 gives 3.333..., 10 // 3 gives 3.

title: bool — True or False
instruction:
A `bool` (boolean) is the simplest type — it can only be `True` or `False`.

Notice: capital T and F. `true` and `false` (lowercase) are not Python booleans.

Create `is_logged_in = True`, `is_premium = False`, `has_errors = False`. Print all three.
hint:
is_logged_in = True
is_premium = False
has_errors = False
starter: # TODO: create three boolean variables and print them

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Booleans are the foundation of every decision in programming. Every if-statement, every loop condition — they all come down to True or False.

title: None — the absence of a value
instruction:
`None` is Python's way of saying "nothing here" or "no value yet".

It is not zero. It is not an empty string. It is the deliberate absence of a value — a placeholder.

Run this. Then add a variable `result = None` and print it.
starter:
user = None
print(user)
print(type(user))
# TODO: add result = None and print it

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: None is not nothing — it is a specific value meaning 'no value'. Functions that do not return anything actually return None. You will see it everywhere.

title: Type inference — Python figures it out
instruction:
Python automatically assigns a type based on the value you give a variable. You never have to declare the type.

`x = 42` → Python makes it an int.
`x = "hello"` → Python makes it a string.

Run this and see the types Python assigned.
starter:
a = 100
b = 3.14
c = "Python"
d = True
e = None

print(type(a).__name__)
print(type(b).__name__)
print(type(c).__name__)
print(type(d).__name__)
print(type(e).__name__)
successMessage: Python inferred all five types from the values — no declarations needed. This is dynamic typing. Fast to write, but it means you must track types mentally.

title: "42" is not 42
instruction:
This is the most common type mistake beginners make.

`"42"` is a string — text that happens to look like a number.
`42` is an integer — an actual number you can do math with.

Run this. The + on strings concatenates (joins). The + on ints adds. They look the same but behave completely differently.
starter:
a = "42"
b = "8"
print(a + b)

c = 42
d = 8
print(c + d)
successMessage: "42" + "8" = "428" (joined text). 42 + 8 = 50 (math). Same operator, completely different behaviour. This trips up every beginner — and now you understand why.

title: Type conversion — int(), float(), str()
instruction:
You can convert between types using `int()`, `float()`, and `str()`.

Fix the broken code below. `age` is a string `"25"` but we need to add 1 to it — which requires converting it to an int first.
starter:
age = "25"
# BUG: can't add 1 to a string — convert age to int first
age = age + 1
print(age)

codeChecks.message: Convert age to int: age = int(age)

successMessage: int() converted the string "25" to the number 25. Then + 1 worked. Type conversion is a daily Python task — input() always returns strings, so you convert constantly.

title: str() — turn anything into a string
instruction:
You cannot concatenate a string and a number directly:

`"Age: " + 25` crashes with a TypeError.

Convert with `str()` first: `"Age: " + str(25)`.

Fix the three broken lines below.
starter:
score = 99
year = 2025
pi = 3.14

# BUG: these will crash — fix them
print("Score: " + score)
print("Year: " + year)
print("Pi: " + pi)
successMessage: str() wraps any value in quotes, making it safe to join with other strings. Though honestly — f-strings (next chapter) are cleaner for this.

title: isinstance() — check the type safely
instruction:
`isinstance(value, type)` returns `True` if a value is of that type.

Better than `type(x) == int` because it also handles subclasses.

Run this. Then add two more `isinstance` checks: one for `score` being an int, one for `name` being a str.
starter:
price = 9.99
name = "Alex"
score = 100

print(isinstance(price, float))
print(isinstance(price, int))
# TODO: check if score is int and if name is str

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: isinstance gives you a clean True/False answer about any value's type. Use it in if-statements to validate data before processing it.

title: Use all five types
instruction:
No starter code.

Create one variable of each type:
- `str` — your name
- `int` — your age
- `float` — a price (e.g. 12.99)
- `bool` — whether you like Python (`True`)
- `None` — a pending result

Print each variable on its own line.
successMessage: Five types, five variables — the complete foundation. Every Python program you ever write will use combinations of these. Chapter 3 done.
